questions.question_text: A landlord verbally agrees to allow a tenant to store equipment in a vacant unit in exchange for reduced rent. This agreement is BEST described as:
questions.explanation: An express contract is one in which the parties have clearly and explicitly stated their agreement, either verbally or in writing. The terms are openly communicated. An implied contract arises from the parties' conduct rather than explicit words — for example, a homeowner who allows a contractor to begin work without a signed agreement may have an implied contract to pay reasonable value. Real estate contracts generally must be in writing to be enforceable under the Statute of Frauds, but they are still 'express' contracts whether written or oral.

questions.question_text: A homeowner calls a plumber to fix a burst pipe without discussing price. The plumber makes the repair. The homeowner is obligated to pay:
questions.correct_answer: A reasonable value for the work
distractors: Whatever the homeowner decides is fair
questions.explanation: An implied contract is formed by the actions and conduct of the parties rather than explicit words or writing. When a homeowner calls a professional for emergency repairs and the professional performs the work, both parties' behavior implies a mutual agreement to pay and be paid at reasonable market rates (quantum meruit — 'as much as deserved'). The absence of a written or verbal price agreement does not eliminate the obligation to pay — it simply means the court will determine reasonable compensation if disputed.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement is a bilateral contract because:
distractors: Only the seller makes a binding promise
Both parties are required to sign, which makes it bilateral by definition
Both parties must perform simultaneously at the closing table
questions.explanation: A bilateral contract involves mutual promises — both parties agree to do something. A listing agreement is bilateral: the seller promises to pay the agreed commission if the broker produces a ready, willing, and able buyer; the broker promises to use reasonable efforts to market the property. This distinguishes it from a unilateral contract, in which only one party makes a promise. A classic real estate unilateral contract is an option — the seller is bound to sell if the buyer exercises the option, but the buyer makes no promise to buy.

questions.question_text: A buyer pays $5,000 for a 90-day option to purchase a property at $300,000. During the option period, only the seller is legally bound. This makes the option agreement a:
questions.explanation: A unilateral contract involves a promise in exchange for an act — one party promises to perform if the other chooses to act. In an option, the seller promises to keep the offer open and sell at the agreed price if the buyer exercises the option. The buyer makes no promise to buy — they have paid for the right to decide. Until the buyer exercises the option, only the seller is bound. Once the buyer exercises it, a bilateral purchase contract is formed. The $5,000 option consideration is typically non-refundable but may apply toward the purchase price.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller have signed a purchase contract. The buyer has not yet secured financing and the seller has not yet delivered clear title. This contract is BEST described as:
questions.explanation: An executory contract is one in which one or both parties still have obligations to perform. A signed purchase contract is executory from the moment of signing until closing — both parties have future duties (buyer to pay and complete inspections; seller to deliver clear title and transfer possession). An executed contract has been fully performed by all parties. Note: a contract can be both bilateral (mutual promises) and executory (not yet performed) — these are not mutually exclusive classifications. Many students confuse 'executed' (fully performed) with 'signed' — signing creates an executory contract, not an executed one.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is an example of an implied contract?
questions.correct_answer: A diner sits down at a restaurant, orders food, and eats
distractors: A buyer and seller sign a written purchase agreement with all terms stated
A landlord and tenant verbally agree to a month-to-month lease at set rent
Two brokers sign a referral agreement specifying the fee percentage
questions.explanation: An implied contract is created by the conduct of the parties rather than by explicit words. When someone sits at a restaurant and orders food, their conduct implies a promise to pay — no written or verbal price agreement is needed. Express contracts, by contrast, have terms clearly stated either orally or in writing. The verbal lease and the written purchase agreement are both express contracts because the parties explicitly communicated their terms.

questions.question_text: Under the Statute of Frauds, most real estate contracts must be in writing to be enforceable. A verbal agreement to sell land is typically:
questions.explanation: The distinction between express/implied and enforceable/unenforceable is important. A verbal agreement to sell land is express (terms were clearly stated) but unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds (it must be in writing to be enforced in court). It is not void — the parties could voluntarily perform it. An implied contract, by contrast, arises from conduct. The Statute of Frauds applies to real estate sales, leases over one year, and other specified contract types.

questions.question_text: A written listing agreement between a seller and a broker is classified as:
questions.explanation: A listing agreement is an express contract because the parties have explicitly stated the terms in writing: the property to be sold, listing price, commission rate, duration, and the broker's duties. Express contracts can be either written or oral, though the Statute of Frauds typically requires listing agreements to be in writing. An implied contract arises from conduct. A quasi-contract is a court-imposed remedy, not a true contract. While listing agreements may be standardized forms, they are negotiable and are not adhesion contracts.

questions.question_text: A property owner tells a real estate agent, 'If you find a buyer who will pay $400,000, I will pay you a 6% commission.' No other promises are exchanged. This is:
questions.explanation: This is a unilateral contract: the owner makes a promise (to pay commission) in exchange for an act (finding a buyer at the specified price). The agent makes no promise to find a buyer — they simply have the opportunity to earn the commission by performing. If the agent finds the buyer, the owner must pay. If the agent does not, the owner owes nothing. Note: in practice, most listing agreements are structured as bilateral contracts where the broker promises to market the property. An open listing, however, is typically unilateral.

questions.question_text: Which statement BEST distinguishes a bilateral contract from a unilateral contract?
questions.correct_answer: Bilateral involves mutual promises; unilateral involves a promise for an act
distractors: Bilateral must be in writing; unilateral can be oral
Bilateral involves real property; unilateral involves personal property
Bilateral is always enforceable; unilateral is never enforceable
questions.explanation: The key distinction is the nature of the exchange. In a bilateral contract, both parties make promises to each other (promise for a promise). In a unilateral contract, one party makes a promise in exchange for the other party's performance of an act (promise for an act). Both types can be written or oral, both can involve any type of property, and both can be enforceable. Most real estate purchase contracts are bilateral — the buyer promises to pay and the seller promises to convey.

questions.question_text: An open listing given to multiple brokers is typically classified as a unilateral contract because:
questions.correct_answer: The seller promises to pay only the broker who produces the buyer
distractors: Only one broker can accept the listing at a time under MLS rules
The seller has made no promise and can cancel without notice
Open listings are not legally binding contracts under any state law
questions.explanation: In an open listing, the seller offers to pay a commission to whichever broker produces a buyer. No single broker makes a promise to market the property — each has the opportunity but not the obligation. This is a classic unilateral structure: promise (commission) for an act (producing a buyer). An exclusive listing, by contrast, is bilateral because the broker promises marketing efforts in exchange for the seller's promise of exclusive compensation. Open listings are binding contracts — the seller must pay the procuring broker.

questions.question_text: At what point does a real estate purchase contract become an executed contract?
distractors: When both buyer and seller have signed the contract
When the buyer's earnest money has been received by escrow
When all contingencies are satisfied but before the closing date
questions.explanation: An executed contract is one in which all obligations have been fully performed by all parties. For a purchase contract, this occurs at closing when the deed is delivered, the purchase price is paid, and possession transfers. Signing the contract makes it binding but executory — performance is still owed. Receiving earnest money is a step in performance but does not complete it. Satisfying contingencies removes conditions but does not complete the sale. The contract remains executory until closing.

questions.question_text: A lease that began on January 1 and ended on December 31 has been fully performed by both landlord and tenant. This lease is now classified as:
questions.explanation: Once both parties have fully performed their obligations — the landlord provided the premises for the lease term, the tenant paid all rent and returned the property — the contract is executed (fully performed). An expired contract and an executed contract are similar but the distinction matters: if the tenant still owes unpaid rent after the term ends, the lease has expired but is not fully executed because obligations remain. Contracts do not become void upon expiration — they are simply completed.

questions.question_text: A real estate transaction has closed: the seller has delivered the deed, the buyer has paid the full purchase price, and nothing remains to be done by either party. The purchase contract is now classified as:
questions.explanation: An executed contract is one that both parties have fully performed. Once closing is complete — the deed delivered and the price paid — no obligations remain, so the contract is executed. A common exam trap: 'executed' in this sense means fully performed, not merely signed. Between signing and closing, the contract is executory, because performance is still owed on both sides. Implied vs. express describes how a contract is created (by conduct vs. by words), and unilateral vs. bilateral describes how it is formed (a promise for an act vs. mutual promises) — neither classification describes performance status.

questions.question_text: Which pair correctly describes the same contract at two different points in time?
questions.correct_answer: Executory before closing, executed after closing
distractors: Bilateral before signing, unilateral after signing
Express before closing, implied after closing
Void before acceptance, valid after acceptance
questions.explanation: A contract's executory/executed classification changes as performance occurs. A purchase contract is executory from the moment of signing (obligations pending) and becomes executed at closing (all obligations fulfilled). However, a contract does not change from bilateral to unilateral or from express to implied — those classifications describe how the contract was formed, not its performance status. A contract is not void before acceptance — it simply does not yet exist as a contract until offer and acceptance occur.
